harness: scripts/loadtest.py — closed-loop httpx over a real socket vs a live uvicorn worker
mode: single uvicorn worker, InMemory StateStore (no Redis/k6 binary in this env)
honest_verdict: Per-request scoring is ~5 ms p99 (c=1), comfortably under the 50 ms target, and stays under it to ~c=4 per worker. A SINGLE Python worker saturates at ~210 rps, so at c=16-32 latency is dominated by queueing (Little's law: latency = concurrency / throughput), NOT by scoring. The README claim is corrected accordingly: p99<50 ms holds per-request and per-worker at realistic concurrency; meeting it under high aggregate load is a HORIZONTAL-SCALE property (N stateless pods behind the externalized StateStore -> N x 210 rps at the same per-request latency), which is exactly why state was externalized. Redis/Kafka multi-pod + a k6 distributed run are the production proof; this is the honest single-node measurement.
